title: Top Five Action Movie Scenes and the Madness of Michael Bay’s ‘Ambulance’
format: Sean Fennessey and Chris Ryan defend Michael Bay's 'Ambulance' as his real comeback, trace what it borrows from Heat to Tenet, and each rank their five favorite action movie sequences of all time.

films.title: Ambulance
films.note: Bay's 15th film and, per the hosts, his official return to form after a decade mostly spent on Transformers sequels. Two adoptive brothers turned bank robbers (Yahya Abdul-Mateen II and Jake Gyllenhaal) hijack an ambulance and take an EMT (Eiza Gonzalez) and a wounded cop hostage for a roughly 90-minute chase across Los Angeles. Both hosts call it the best chase movie since Fury Road, praise the drone photography and Lorne Balfe's score, and rate it a strong theatrical recommendation despite a thin script and an overstuffed back half of characters.

segments.heading: The Bay Filmography
segments.note: what Ambulance borrows from, and what it answers

films.title: 6 Underground
films.note: Netflix action film the hosts saw theatrically and loved as a bracing, hilarious spy adventure; most people watching at home thought it was noisy bullshit.

films.title: Transformers
films.note: Sean still defends the first one as a worthwhile CGI experiment, before Bay spent several more years and four sequels on the franchise.

films.title: The Rock
films.note: Cited with Bad Boys as proof Bay's real strength is smaller, urban-set thrillers rather than franchise spectacle. Ambulance gives a supporting character the same name, Willy Sharpe, as William Fichtner's here, and has characters mistake the movie for the actor it's also named after.

films.title: Armageddon
films.note: Chris says Ambulance has an 'engine,' meaning a propulsive plot device, that Bay's films have mostly lacked since this one.

films.title: Pain & Gain
films.note: Where Bay first worked with Dwayne Johnson, a.k.a. 'The Rock.'

films.title: Heat
films.note: One of three L.A. crime movies, along with Collateral and Training Day, that Ambulance overtly references.

films.title: Tenet
films.note: Sean reads Ambulance's tighter, more legible chase geography as Bay's response to Tenet's.

films.title: Speed
films.note: Chris's comparison for the movie's disregard for practical logistics: the ambulance's gas tank never runs low.

segments.heading: Gyllenhaal's Unhinged Era
segments.note: the 10-year anniversary of the movie that changed his career

films.title: End of Watch
films.note: Sean's demarcation point for Gyllenhaal deciding to take the safety off as an actor; also an L.A.-set movie largely shot inside cars, like Ambulance.

films.title: Stronger
films.note: The Boston Marathon bombing drama neither host could name off the top of his head.

films.title: Spider-Man: Far From Home
films.note: Where Gyllenhaal played Mysterio.

films.title: Wedding Crashers
films.note: Kier O'Donnell, who plays an FBI agent in Ambulance, is instantly recognizable to the hosts as the younger Cleary brother obsessed with Vince Vaughn's character here, one of the episode's favorite pieces of stunt casting.

segments.heading: Chris's Top Five Action Movie Scenes
segments.note: ranked best to worst; stunt work required
segments.subject: action movie scenes

films.title: The Dark Knight
films.note: No. 1: the Joker's prisoner-transfer truck flip. Chris's pick for the purest distillation of what makes an action sequence work: real stakes, a plan that shouldn't work, and a stunt that still looks inexplicable to him.

films.title: Mad Max: Fury Road
films.note: No. 2: a two-for-one 'cheat' pick, paired with the tanker-assault chase from Mad Max 2: The Road Warrior. Chris rates George Miller among the great car-chase directors, alongside Peckinpah and Frankenheimer.

films.title: Aliens
films.note: No. 3: the Marines' first encounter with the hive. Picked for sound and tension rather than spectacle: an action sequence that goes right up to the edge of action.

films.title: The Raid 2
films.note: No. 4: the prison-riot sequence, which Chris calls maybe the most violent thing on either list.

films.title: Hard Boiled
films.note: No. 5: John Woo's hospital-hallway shootout, which Chris calls a summation of every shootout before it and a preview of John Wick and The Matrix after.

segments.heading: Sean's Top Five Action Movie Scenes
segments.note: ranked best to worst; stunt work required
segments.subject: action movie scenes

films.title: Kill Bill: Vol. 1
films.note: No. 1: the Bride vs. the Crazy 88s, a 35-minute sequence Sean calls his cheat pick because of its length. Loaded with homage: Bruce Lee's yellow tracksuit, Shaw Brothers wire work, Gordon Liu as one of the 88s.

films.title: Terminator 2: Judgment Day
films.note: No. 2: the T-1000's LA River Basin truck chase. Sean's pick for the moment 1990s action iconography actually begins, and the template for a generation of homages and parodies.

films.title: Jurassic Park
films.note: No. 3: the T. rex's escape. Cited for blending practical Stan Winston creature work with CGI so well it still holds up 30 years later.

films.title: Oldboy
films.note: No. 4: the single-take hammer-hallway fight. Chosen for choreography so precise it plays as documentary rather than staged action.

films.title: Mission: Impossible
films.note: No. 5: the Langley computer-hacking break-in. A De Palma sequence Sean picked for pure tension with no guns involved, capped by Tom Cruise suspended over the vault floor.

segments.heading: Honorable Mentions
segments.note: scenes that almost made the cut

films.title: The Matrix
films.note: Left off both lists only for having too many candidate scenes; Sean's favorite is the lobby shootout.

films.title: Die Hard
films.note: Possibly the best action movie ever made, but neither host could isolate one signature sequence from the whole.

films.title: The Enforcer
films.note: A good Eastwood Dirty Harry entry, but Sean says its shootouts don't hold up next to Heat's.

films.title: Saving Private Ryan
films.note: The Omaha Beach opening, set aside along with other large-scale battle sequences as 'kind of cheating' since so much of that scale leans on VFX.

films.title: The Lord of the Rings: The Two Towers
films.note: The Battle of Helm's Deep, grouped with the other battle sequences left off both lists.

films.title: Star Wars: Episode I - The Phantom Menace
films.note: Chris's answer for a Star Wars pick: the Duel of the Fates lightsaber duel, from a movie he agrees isn't very good.

films.title: Return of the Jedi
films.note: Sean's answer: the prison-barge escape.

films.title: Solo: A Star Wars Story
films.note: Chris's actual favorite Star Wars action beat: the train robbery that reveals Lando's backstory.

films.title: Children of Men
films.note: The opening car-ambush sequence, another one that felt 'a little odd' to include but got a mention anyway.

films.title: Apocalypse Now
films.note: The Ride of the Valkyries helicopter assault, raised near the end as an obvious omission.

films.title: The Fast and the Furious: Tokyo Drift
films.note: Chris's favorite entry in a franchise he's otherwise seen exactly one movie of.

referenced.group: Also cited in the top fives
